item.content:encoded: Where has this page been all along, oh boy. I would definitely ask myself to take time and go through this again, after being shocked(?) by the final advertisement. And TIL some German too, and I think one of my native German friends will enjoy this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you very much, I keep every nice thing (and uh maybe the occasional nasty thing) people say about my writing in a special part of my brain that only stops working half the time. The other parts have a much worse success rate.

I'm very glad it all meant something to you :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A veritable work of art. Don't forget this- as a message for myself and you. +1

item.title: Re: most intriguing & thought-provoking scp i've seen in a while
item.content:encoded:
Wow! That's… the nicest comment I've gotten about anything I've written! Everything you said about my piece is exactly what I wanted it to be. I feel as excited as you are because of those kind words, thank you ever so very much.

(I think the term is 'article', but terminology varies.)

I'm really glad it resonated personally with you, and if you need any help with that translation, just shoot me a PM on Wikidot! Thanks again for leaving your comment, it means more than you can know.

item.title: most intriguing & thought-provoking scp i've seen in a while
item.content:encoded:
i literally (sorry for using this word, i knew how tiresomely overused it is in the spoken American English but still i'm way too excited :D just picture a teenager boy seeing Eminem for the first time
or whoever his idol might be :P) joined the SCP site for this file.
i'm a Chinese STEM students trying to get into our university's Master in Interpretation program, and i'm just overwhelmingly moved when i finished reading this file, about how words matter and how the thirst for peace and truth and the scientific rigor(i learned this watching TBBT lol)inspired this story.
This is possibly my favourite novel (essay? i'm not sure what the right term is for this :/) i've read since i became fascinated by English literature.
I tried to get my friends who aren't even familiar with SCP to read this and hit plenty of bumps, i'll see if i can translate this to Chinese if possible(btw still trying to figure out how to do that, maybe i can first translate part of the text)
I loved how the pseudoscience element of the story played out and as a Chinese i sure know a lot about pseudoscience from growing up(as well as from watching american TVs and learning about crystals that'll fix diarrhea and chemical-filled patches that heal you while doing so)
Really looking forward to seeing more

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your position is clear. It was already clear. It has been responded to, and I have no further response. Please move on.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Per the post above, this is a general reminder to everyone to please keep discussion civil moving forward.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
Quasibenevolence, please do not make personal accusations against other users. If you feel that someone has broken site guidelines and is trolling, notify a staff member to address the situation rather than (what seems to be) calling someone who disagrees with you a troll.

As a general reminder to everyone, please keep discussion civil moving forward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Typos aren’t contagious. Further, the fora aren’t equivalent. If a high-end museum exhibit has sticky floors and smells bad, that’s a problem for the display. If the museum’s janitor’s closet has those traits but none of the exhibits do, that’s fine.

Most of my effort was spent in trying to express how bad undermining a really important phrase concerning logical fallacies was in a way that wouldn’t pick up trolls. This effort was unsuccessful; scroll down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're going to do this, please do it somewhere other than my article's discussion board.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Literally addressed in the post you replied to.

You're right. Every comment on War and Peace and the Bible that is less than doesn't bust a bookshelf itself is invalid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, that's high praise! Thank you very kindly indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That final interview (along with the letters) was some of the best dialogue I've ever read in an SCP.

+1

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: ETA: addressed, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seven thousand words and your critique is focused on three?

Yall ever heard of missing the forest for the trees?

language is alive and changes with society’s use of it… and if society uses the language in a given fashion it is no longer “incorrect.”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not one of my personal bugbears, but as a university course instructor I do have several dozen of them, so I've gone and raised the question instead to ease your mind.

Occasionally I think about making an SCP about how people who otherwise wouldn't make typos will make typos when correcting other people's grammar and spelling and word use, i.e. "even the demand one to question." The virus gets us all, in the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Said addresses never corresponded to actual physical locations, begging the question of how the mail was delivered at all.

It may well prompt one to ask the question, or inspire the question, or even the demand one to question, but, as it is not a reference to a particularly logical fallacy, it does not beg the question.

I know english-speakers are grinding this misuse into proper use, but the error still irks me and I will die on this hill. A nonfictional virus within the language one must cure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: High praise from the author of my favourite canon. Thanks for much for the compliments, I'm pumped that it resonated with you so much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This deserves far more upvotes than it has currently. Everyone, get in here and read what's probably one of the best SCPs I've read in the last six months.

item.title: Re: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
What a time to be alive.

Your work is, as always, much appreciated!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! Feedback like that keeps writers writing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was the kind of quality content that keeps me coming back to the Foundation. Thoroughly enjoyable.

item.title: Re: finally an explanation for that soap
item.content:encoded:
Spread the word! Pseudoscience is only good for curing skeleton fire.

Thank you for the kind words, I'm glad the effort shone through! Hope you don't hate everything else I wrote.

item.title: finally an explanation for that soap
item.content:encoded:
i'm going to send this article to my brother who always uses that soap so he understands its true origin.

brilliant work, the time and effort you put into this is very apparent. now i need to read the rest odf your writings.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The link to the Toronto Archives picture expired, because that's a thing apparently. Author has confirmed with me in PMs that the image is Public Domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I put a lot into this, and I'm glad it worked out; I'm also extraordinarily relieved to have it out of the way :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. Those months were well spent. These really in depth articles are such a joy to read, but are also not as common, so finding a good one like this is truly special. Can't wait to see what's next!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I hope so too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the depth you put into this SCP, I hope a lot of others get the chance to read it.

item.title: It's been a long haul for all concerned
item.content:encoded:
The medium is the message.
- Marshall McLuhan

I've been working on this for months.

I've written and published two tales totalling about twenty-three thousand words and a joke SCP in that time. I've also written and not published three shorter tales and another complete SCP (which is now likely imminent). This idea grew in the execution, and it's been both rewarding and infuriating watching it get away from me then trying to rein it back in.

In the end, you can only rein so much. Dr. Zwist knows that all too well.

Thanks to Grigori Karpin and his excellent, conscientious crit for forcing me to rein it in further than I thought I could! I will fight you if you think there are still extraneous sections to this. You should see my cutting room floor! Because you can't.

Things What Might Look Like Mistakes but Aren't:
1. Lots of Thaumiels are compound anomalies;
2. Hiemal anomalies contain each other. This one does not. It's not Hiemal;
3. Yes, SCP-006 is Overseer-eyes only. Seriously. Go look (or don't, yawn);
4. No, you're not meant to be able to read all the text on a few of these pics. What can be reasonably faked at 300px will look like hot garbage at 500, when said faker is amateur at best;
5. Uhhhh… they did have metal bottle caps in 1895!

+ Image Credits
- Close

I'm so sorry.

Brumley.jpg, Clio.jpg, Eye.jpg, Polk.jpg, Reiki.jpg, Trick.jpg, and Washburn.jpg are the author's work, freely released under the CC 3.0 license.

Ditto the bottle photograph used in Bromide.jpg.

Many of these images are replacements because the source of the originals decided to suck. Images that need CC verification are indicated in blue. Everything else has already been verified.

AngelaMercy.jpg is a composite. Its components were borrowed from:

Bottle:
Original Source: Zorger
Creator: Unknown
License: Public Domain

Figures:
Original Source: Library and Archives Canada
Creator: Basil Hall Collection
NOTE: "Droit d'auteur : Expiré" is the relevant information in this citation - the copyright has expired.

Billboard:
Original Source: City of Toronto Archives
Creator: E.L. Ruddy Company
License: Public Domain

The portrait in Braun.jpg is borrowed from:

Original Source: Library and Archives Canada
Creator: Unknown
License: Public Domain

The portrait in Bromide.jpg is borrowed from:

Original Source: Library and Archives Canada
Creator: Morris Norman Collection
License: Public Domain

The woodcut in Codex.jpg is borrowed from:

Original Source: Wikimedia Commons
Creator: Unknown
License: Public Domain

The photograph in Heino.jpg is borrowed from:

Original Source: flickr
Creator: FailedImitator
License: CC-BY SA

Mercy.jpg is borrowed from:

Original Source: Wikimedia Commons
Creator: collezione Luca Cristini
License: Public Domain

Scout1.jpg is borrowed from:

Original Source: Museums Victoria
Creator: Museums Victoria
License: CC 4.0

Scout2.jpg is borrowed from:

Original Source: Museums Victoria
Creator: Museums Victoria
License**: Public Domain

ScoutLake.jpg is a composite. Its components were borrowed from:

Flower
Original Source: flickr
Creator: Swami Stream
License: CC BY 2.0

Pills
Original Source: Wikimedia Commons

License: CC Public Domain

License: CC BY 2.0
